coredata.dc:title: Enhanced neuroprotective effect of verapamil-loaded hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots in an in-vitro model of amyloid-induced Alzheimer's disease

coredata.dc:description: This study aims to investigate the molecular mechanisms and the neuroprotective effect of hyaluronic acid modified verapamil-loaded carbon quantum dots (VRH-loaded HA-CQDs) against an in-vitro Alzheimer's disease model induced by amyloid beta (Aβ) in SH-SY5Y and Neuro 2a neuroblastoma cells. Briefly, different HA-CQDs were prepared using hydrothermal method and optimized by Box-Behnken design to maximize quantum yield and minimize particle size. Serum stable negatively charged VRH-loaded HA-CQDs was successfully prepared by admixing the optimized HA-CQDs and VRH with association efficiency and loading capacity of 81.25 ± 3.65 % and 5.11 ± 0.81 %, respectively. Cells were pretreated with VRH solution or loaded-HA-CQDs followed by exposure to Aβ. Compared to the control group, amyloidosis led to reduction in cellular proliferation, mitochondrial membrane potential, expression of cytochrome P450, cytochrome c oxidase, CREB-regulated transcriptional coactivator 3, and mitotic index, along with marked increase in reactive oxygen species (ROS) and inflammatory cytokines. Pretreatment with VRH, either free or loaded HA-CQDs, enhanced cell survival, mitochondrial membrane potential, mitotic index, and gene expression. It also reduced inflammation and ROS. However, VRH-loaded HA-CQDs exhibited superior effectiveness in the measured parameters. These findings suggest that VRH-loaded HA-CQDs have enhanced therapeutic potential compared to free VRH in mitigating amyloidosis negative features.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 3D response plot between different CPPs on HA-CQDs quantum yield %. AB, AD and CD have an antagonistic effect on quantum yield %. AC, BC and BD have direct effect on quantum yield %.
3D response plot between different CPPs on HA-CQDs particle size. AB, AC, AD, BC and CD have an antagonistic effect on particle size. BD has a direct effect on particle size.

The effect of serum incubation on optimized VRH loaded HA-CQDs particle size, PDI and zeta potential. The selected VRH loaded HA-CQDs were incubated with 0 %, 10 % and 50 % FBS for 4, 24 and 48 h then particle size (a), PDI (b) and zeta potential (c) were measured using DLS as described. Data points represent mean and SD (n = 3). Statistical analysis was carried out using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey HSD test and p < 0.05 was considered significant. Serum protein had a non-significant effect on VRH loaded HA-CQDs particle size, PDI or zeta potential.

In vitro characterization of the optimized VRH loaded HA-CQDs. Transmission electron micrograph of VRH loaded HA-CQDs (a). The VRH loaded HA-CQDs appeared as spherical nanostructure with particle size in consistency with the DLS technique. In vitro release profile of VRH from the optimized HA-CQDs in PBS (pH 7.4) in the presence of FBS (50 % v/v) at 37 °C (b). Drug release was measured by dialyzing HA-CQDs in the presence of 50 % FBS against PBS (pH 7.4). Drug concentration in the dialysate was assessed by HPLC. Data point represents mean and SD (n = 3).

The in vitro hemolysis assay of the optimized VRH loaded HA-CQDs. Rat RBCs were incubated with VRH loaded HA-CQDs at different concentrations (10–50%w/v) for 2 h at 37 °C. Positive and negative controls were 0.5 w/v% Triton X-100 and PBS (pH 7.4), respectively. Samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C and the absorbance of the released haemoglobin was determined at 545 nm. Data point represents mean and SD (n = 3). The dotted line represents the acceptable hemolysis range.

Comparative cellular viability of SH-Sy5y cells exposed to different concentrations of free VRH, HA-CQDs, and VRH loaded HA-CQDs. (a) free VRH, (b) HA-CQDs, (c) VRH loaded HA-CQDs, and (d) free vs. loaded VRH. 3.75 μg/ mL was the safest concentration. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05. a: significant vs. control and b: significant vs. Aβ control. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Comparative cellular viability of Neuro 2a cells exposed to different concentrations of free VRH, HA-CQDs, and VRH loaded HA-CQDs. (a) free VRH, (b) HA-CQDs, (c) VRH loaded HA-CQDs, and (d) free vs. loaded VRH. 3.75 μg/ mL was the safest concentration. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05. a: significant vs. control and b: significant vs. Aβ control. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Comparative cytotoxicity of SH-Sy5y cells exposed to different concentrations of free VRH, HA-CQDs, and VRH loaded HA-CQDs. (a) free VRH, (b) HA-CQDs, (c) VRH loaded HA-CQDs, and (d) free vs. loaded VRH. 3.75 μg/ mL was the safest concentration. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05. a: significant vs. control and b: significant vs. Aβ control. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Comparative cytotoxicity of Neuro 2a cells exposed to different concentrations of free VRH, HA-CQDs, and VRH loaded HA-CQDs. (a) free VRH, (b) HA-CQDs, (c) VRH loaded HA-CQDs, and (d) free vs. loaded VRH. 3.75 μg/ mL was the safest concentration. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05. a: significant vs. control and b: significant vs. Aβ control. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Effect on inflammatory cytokines. (a) TNF-α SH-Sy5y cells F (4, 15) = 38.290, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.939, (b) IL-6 SH-Sy5y cells, F (4, 15) = 50.091, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.952, (c) TNF-α Neuro 2a cells, F (4, 15) = 312.153, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.992, and (d) IL-6 Neuro 2a cells, F (4, 15) = 112.814, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.978. Data are presented as mean, n = 3. a: significant vs. control, b: significant vs. Aβ, c: significant vs. Free VRH, and d: significant vs. HA-CQDs. TNF-α: tumor necrosis factor alpha, Il-6: interleukin 6, Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Effect on reactive oxygen species. (a) fluorescent signals of SH-Sy5y cells, (b) heatmap presentation for CTCF of SH-Sy5y cells, F (4, 15) = 93.367, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.974, (c) fluorescent signals of Neuro 2a cells, and (d) heatmap presentation for CTCF of Neuro 2a cells, F (4, 15) = 61.199, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.961. Data are presented as mean, n = 3, p < 0.05. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Effect on mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP). (a) fluorescent signals of SH-Sy5y cells, (b) heatmap presentation for MMP of SH-Sy5y cells, F (4, 15) = 6.991, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.737, (c) fluorescent signals of Neuro 2a cells, and (d) heatmap presentation for MMP of Neuro 2a cells, F (4, 15) = 24.992, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.909. Data are presented as mean, n = 3. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Heatmap presentation of the detected genes in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells. CYP: cytochrome P450, CCO: cytochrome C oxidase, CRTC3: CREB-regulated transcriptional coactivator 3, Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

Effect on mitotic index (MI). (a) photomicrograph of SH-Sy5y chromosomes, (b) MI of SH-Sy5y cells, F (4, 15) = 169.603, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.985, (c) photomicrograph of Neuro 2a chromosomes, and (d) MI of Neuro 2a cells, F (4, 15) = 138.894, p < 0.0001, η2 = 0.982. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05. Aβ: amyloid beta protein, VRH: free verapamil hydrochloride, HA-CQDs: hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to investigate the molecular mechanisms and the neuroprotective effect of hyaluronic acid modified verapamil-loaded carbon quantum dots (VRH-loaded HA-CQDs) against an in-vitro Alzheimer's disease model induced by amyloid beta (Aβ) in SH-SY5Y and Neuro 2a neuroblastoma cells. Briefly, different HA-CQDs were prepared using hydrothermal method and optimized by Box-Behnken design to maximize quantum yield and minimize particle size. Serum stable negatively charged VRH-loaded HA-CQDs was successfully prepared by admixing the optimized HA-CQDs and VRH with association efficiency and loading capacity of 81.25 ± 3.65 % and 5.11 ± 0.81 %, respectively. Cells were pretreated with VRH solution or loaded-HA-CQDs followed by exposure to Aβ. Compared to the control group, amyloidosis led to reduction in cellular proliferation, mitochondrial membrane potential, expression of cytochrome P450, cytochrome c oxidase, CREB-regulated transcriptional coactivator 3, and mitotic index, along with marked increase in reactive oxygen species (ROS) and inflammatory cytokines. Pretreatment with VRH, either free or loaded HA-CQDs, enhanced cell survival, mitochondrial membrane potential, mitotic index, and gene expression. It also reduced inflammation and ROS. However, VRH-loaded HA-CQDs exhibited superior effectiveness in the measured parameters. These findings suggest that VRH-loaded HA-CQDs have enhanced therapeutic potential compared to free VRH in mitigating amyloidosis negative features.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disorder that represents about 60–70 % of overall dementias [1,2]. According to World Health Organization, AD and dementia-related illness are the 7th leading cause of death and one of the main causes of disabilities among elderly people. Consequently, AD and other dementias have negative impact on many aspects such as mental, social, and economical states of the patients themselves, their families, and also their care givers [1,2]. Therefore, much more attention is required and fetching for therapeutic modalities is an urgent demand.

In order to explore novel neuroprotective agents, the pathophysiology and the underlying molecular mechanisms should be investigated. Multiple hypotheses have been suggested to be implicated in the development of AD including, but not limited to, aggregates of amyloid beta (Aβ) neurotoxic plaques, accumulated hyper-phosphorylated tau protein, oxidative stress, over-activated microglial cells with subsequent inflammation, and downregulated cAMP response element-binding protein (CREB) [3].

More specifically, AD is marked by activated amyloidogenic pathway in which the intracellular amyloid precursor protein (APP) undergoes successive cleavage by β- and γ-secretases that liberate the insoluble Aβ, which further accumulates and forms neurotoxic plaques. Deposition of Aβ activates microglial cells, elicits inflammatory response, stimulates the release of inflammatory mediators, and eventually causes neuronal cell death [4]. It has been also found that Aβ can enter the mitochondrion, induce oxidative stress, and release the reactive oxygen species (ROS), which in turn, causes mitochondrial dysfunction; impaired electron transport chain (ETC); dysregulated calcium homeostasis; and irreversible cellular injury [5]. More specifically, Aβ restrains the mitochondrial respiration by blocking the activity of cytochrome c oxidase (CCO) or complex IV [6].

Aβ can also interfere with the expression of CREB, which is an important protein for neuronal plasticity and for memory formation and consolidation. CREB signaling also resolves synaptic loss caused by Aβ aggregates [7]. The downregulated CREB pathway has been found to affect the mitochondrial biogenesis and its related functions [8]. Furthermore, CREB-regulated transcriptional coactivator 3 (CRTC3) is a member of the CRTCs family proteins that can modulate CREB transcription in response to cAMP and calcium signals [9].

The imbalanced calcium homeostasis caused by Aβ aggregates suggests voltage-dependent calcium channel (VDCC) blockers as neuro-protective candidates. Verapamil (VRH) is a well-known L-type calcium channel blocker that has been approved for the treatment of cardiovascular disorders. Moreover, VRH has been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity, which may overcome the oxidative stress-induced AD [10].

There is no doubt that prevention measures can slow the onset and the progression of the disease since there is no a distinct curative therapy [11]. Unfortunately, Most of the available treatment strategies have a partial and temporary effect because they only affect the consequences and the symptoms of the disease rather than modifying the underlying cause. Moreover, drug delivery to the brain represents a major challenge owing to blood brain barrier (BBB), which forces the healthcare givers to increase the dose with subsequent undesirable side effects [12]. Nevertheless, recent research found that nano-systems can easily cross BBB and offer advantages over the conventional therapies [13].

Recently, nanotechnology has been extensively employed in the biomedical field in order to improve early diagnosis, therapeutics, and management of several diseases [14,15]. Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconducting inorganic nanoparticles with unique optical characters. They have several advantages including, but not limited to, lower toxicity, ease of formulation and processing, and long-term standing in biological fluids. Their stability, safety, solubility are major concerns when they are designated for biomedical applications [16]. There are different types of QDs based on the core element, which can be cadmium; silver; indium; carbon; or silicon. In addition to the core element, there is a shell that allow surface modification and bio-conjugation, which is a key factor in their character and biosafety.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm that have distinct optical and electronic properties [17]. CQDs are more advantageous than simple metal QDs and organic dyes owing to their high water solubility, high stability against photo bleaching, superior biocompatibility, and low toxicity [18–21]. CQDs represent a very useful drug delivery system owing to their small size and biocompatibility, which allow them to cross blood brain barrier (BBB); the major challenging point in the treatment of AD [22].

We previously investigated the neuroprotective effect of VRH, which was mediated through modulation of calcium-dependent genes concomitantly with decreasing the phosphorylated tau protein and also through its anti-inflammatory property [3]. However, this study aims to (1) examine the neuroprotective effect of verapamil-loaded CQDs compared to the free drug on Aβ-induced AD-like in-vitro model using SH-Sy5y human neuroblastoma and Neuro 2a mouse neuroblastoma cell lines, and (2) explore the mechanistic insights through which verapamil could exert its neuroprotection such as modulation of the accumulated Aβ, oxidative stress, inflammation, and other genetic aspects, which could affect the pathogenesis of the disease.

VRH loaded HA-CQDs is thought to be a novel approach that combines the therapeutic effects of VRH with the targeted delivery and imaging capabilities of CQDs, enhanced by hyaluronic acid (HA) modification. This innovative system offers several advantages over using VRH or CQDs separately in the treatment of AD. VRH can cross the BBB to some extent but with no evidence for specific targeting to neuronal cells [23,24]. Consequently, its efficiency is limited resulting in suboptimal therapeutic levels in the brain. Therefore, high doses required to achieve therapeutic effects in the brain that may cause systemic side effects. On the other hand, HA-CQDs can penetrate the BBB due to their small size and surface properties; allowing for more efficient delivery of VRH to the brain [25]. Moreover, HA targets CD44 receptors, which are overexpressed in various cell types, including certain brain cells and microglia associated with neuroinflammation in AD [26]. These enhancements collectively improve the therapeutic efficacy and safety of verapamil in the treatment of AD; marking a notable advancement in neurotherapeutic strategies.

Hyaluronic acid sodium salt from Streptococcus equi (HA), ascorbic acid, methanol (HPLC grade), acetonitrile (HPLC grade), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Company, UK. Verapamil hydrochloride (> 99 %) was obtained from Abcam, UK. Potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate and o-phosphoric acid were purchased from Fluka Chemika BioChemika, Switzerland. EMEM; Ham's F-12; and RPMI cell culture media, biotin-Aβ(1 – 42) protein, fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin/ streptomycin, phosphate buffered saline (PBS), trypsin-EDTA, and trypan blue were purchased from Elabscience® (USA). 1,1,1,3,3,3-Hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP) was from Santa Cruz Biotechnology (USA). Colcemid solution was from SERANA (Germany). Other chemicals were of high grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots
Preparation of hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Different hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots (HA-CQDs) were prepared using single step hydrothermal method [27]. Initially, a precursor solution was prepared by dissolving HA (1, 2 and 3 % w/v) and ascorbic acid (2, 3 and 4 % w/v) as mentioned in Table S1 in deionized water at room temperature under magnetic stirring (500 rpm) for 1 h until both components are completely dissolved. The mixture solution (10 mL) was transferred to a 25 mL stainless-steel autoclave vessel. The vessel was heated in furnace to different temperature values namely, 180, 200 and 220 °C for different time intervals (2, 4 and 6 h) according to the experimental design. After cooling down to room temperature overnight, the obtained yellowish brown aqueous dispersion was purified by ultrafiltration centrifugation using Amicon® Ultra filters (3500 D) at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, a clear yellowish aqueous filtrate containing HA-CQDs was collected. Different HA-CQDs were purified by dialysis against 1 L deionized water (MWCO, 1000D) for 24 h. Water was changed every 6 h to remove any small impurities. Consequently solid HA-CQDs powders were obtained by lyophilization for 48 h and stored in tightly closed amber glass vials for further characterization [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Different HA-CQDs were optimized using Box-Behnken design (BBD). Design-Expert software (Design-Expert 13.0.14, State-Ease Inc., USA) was implemented to creäte matrix and investigate both the response surfaces and the statistical models [29]. The selected critical process parameters (CPPs) under this study were HA and ascorbic acid concentrations, as well as reaction temperature and time and were coded as A, B, C, and D respectively (Table S1). The optimized HA-CQDs was designed to maximize both quantum yield (Y1) and particle size (Y2). Table S2 lists the 29 different formulae designed by the software. The design space was created to specify the optimum CPPs to prepare optimized HA-CQDs with the desired quality target product profile (QTPP) based on the highest desirability [29]. One optimum checkpoint was picked for validation of the chosen domain and equations. The experimental values of the responses were quantitatively compared with that of the predicted values and prediction error (%) calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of verapamil loaded hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: The fabricated optimized HA-CQDs were loaded with verapamil hydrochloride (VRH, abcam, UK) by admixing HA-CQDs (40 mg/mL) and VRH (20 mg/mL) in deionized water. The obtained mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min at room temperature. Consequently, the VRH loaded HA-CQDs were shaken a thermostatically controlled shaking water bath at 250 Stroke ±0.1 at 25 ± 0.5 °C for 24 h (Thermostatic Shaker, Daihan Labtech Shaker Water Bath, LSB 030S, Shaking speed 20 to 250 Stroke/ min with temperature range: ambient +5 to 99 °C, South Korea). Different VRH loaded HA-CQDs were dialyzed (MWCO, 1000D) against deionized water for 1 h to remove free unadsorbed VRH.

Quantum yield of the prepared HA-CQDs and VRH loaded CQDs was quantified using quinine sulfate as a reference [30]. Five concentrations of each formula (0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5 μg/mL) in deionized water (refractive index, η of 1.33) and quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) were prepared. The fluorescence spectra of each sample were recorded at λ excitation of 340 nm and the integrated photoluminescence intensities (excited at 340 nm) were plotted against the absorbance values at 340 nm also. Quantum yield was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ ST

ST
where Φ is the quantum yield, ΦST = 0.54, m is slope, η is the refractive index of the solvent, ST is the standard and X is the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of particle size and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size and size distribution, expressed as polydispersity index (PDI) of different HA-CQDs with and without VRH, were measured using dynamic light scattering (particle size analyser, Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Zeta potential was quantified using electrophoresis. Briefly, different formulations were diluted using deionized water (1: 100 v/v) and transferred to disposable plain folded capillary Zeta cells. All the measurements represent the average of 20 runs, each run was completed in triplicate at 25 °C [31].

The association efficiency percentage (AE%) of VRH was quantified by dissolving the prepared VRH loaded HA-CQDs (5 mg) into methanol (≥99.9 % HPLC grade, 25 mL) [28]. Subsequently, the amount of associated VRH was measured using HPLC [32]. Briefly, VRH was quantified using a reverse phase C18 column (Supelcosil LC-18, 250 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm) was used at 25 °C. The mobile phase consisted of 0.05 M potassium dihydrogen phosphate: acetonitrile: o-phosphoric acid (69.5:30:0.5) (pH 3.5). The elute flow rate was 1 mL/min which was monitored at a wavelength of 276 nm. The AE% and the percentage of loading capacity (LC%) is calculated using the following equations:
(2)
AE % =
amount of
VRH
inside the
HA − CQDs
pellets

∗ 100
(3)

The fabricated VRH HA-CQD was studied by measuring the particle size, PDI and zeta potential after incubation with 10 % and 50 % v/v fetal bovine serum (FBS) for 4, 24, and 48 h at 37 ± 0.5 °C. The measurements were made as described before.

The optimized VRH loaded HA-CQDs in deionized water was placed on a copper 300-mesh grid, coated with carbon and allowed to stand for 10 min; then, any excess fluid was absorbed by filter paper. The sample was stained with one drop of 1 % phosphotungstic acid and dried for 5 min then imaged with TEM (Jeol, JEM-1230, Japan) at 200 KV.

The drug release from the optimized VRH loaded HA-CQDs was performed using the dialysis membrane method with slight modifications [27]. An aliquot volume of the prepared VRH loaded HA-CQDs (equivalent to 5 mg VRH) was placed in the pre-soaked dialysis membrane (cut off: 1000 Da) diluted with 1 mL of PBS (pH 7.4), mixed with FBS (at final 50 % v/v). The tightly closed membranes were put in containers filled with 50 mL PBS (pH 7.4), in a thermostatically controlled shaking water bath at 250 strokes/min ± 0.1 at 37 ± 0.5 °C. At predetermined time intervals, 0.5 mL aliquots were withdrawn and replaced by fresh PBS. The drug in the samples was analyzed using HPLC as previously described and the percentage of VRH released was calculated [32].

The hemolytic activity of the chosen VRH loaded HA-CQDs was assessed using fresh male albino rat's RBCs. Briefly, blood was collected on heparinized tube from male albino rat (aged 2–3 months, 200 g ± 10 %) tail vein (1 mL from each animal). The blood was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to collect the RBCs. The RBCs were incubated with different concentrations of the optimized formula and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. The samples were then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. The absorbance of each supernatant was determined at 545 nm. The RBCs were incubated with 0.5 w/v% Triton X-100 as a positive control and PBS (pH 7.4) as a negative control [33]. The % hemolysis was calculated using the following equation:
(4)
% Hemolysis =
absorbance sample − absorbance negative control
absorbance positive control − absorbance negative control
∗ 100

SH-Sy5y human cell line (AcceGen Biotech Cat.# ABC-TC1019, USA) and Neuro 2a mouse neuroblastoma cell line (ABC-TC0819, USA) were used in our study to ensure validity of the results and to broad the findings. The cells were grown in EMEM culture media supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/ streptomycin. SH-Sy5y media was supplemented with extra Ham's F-12 nutrient mixture. The cells were maintained at 5 % CO2 and 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and visualization of Aβ(1 – 42) protein
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of Aβ(1 – 42) protein (Cat. No. E-PP-0459, Elabscience®, USA) was done according to Melone, M.A.B., et al. (2018) with minor modifications [34] to induce an in-vitro model for AD. Briefly, the protein was dissolved in 1,1,1,3,3,3-Hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP, Santa Cruz Biotechnology, USA) to prepare a stock solution of 100 μM concentration. HFIP was allowed to evaporate and the biotinylated protein was re-dissolved in a total volume of 1000 μL; 100 μL sodium hydroxide (50 mM) and 900 μL PBS. Afterward, the solution was left in ice for 2 h, followed by centrifugation for 30 s and was filtered by a 0.2 μm filter. The solution was diluted to 25 μM concentration. Finally, the Aβ protein was ready to be added to the cells.

For visualization of the prepared Aβ protein, the same procedures were performed till ice incubation. Then, 100 μL was incubated for one hour with 6 × 103 of SH-Sy5y cells and of Neuro 2a cells. 100 μL horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-streptavidin was then incubated with the cells for 90 min to conjugate with the biotinylated Aβ protein. The unconjugated peptides were removed by using Pierce™ Dye Removal Columns (Cat. No. 22858, Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA). Afterward, 90 μL of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was added as a substrate for HRP-streptavidin and the fluorescent signals were detected (Fig. S1) by the red filter of EVOS™ FLoid™ Cell Imaging Station (Thermo Fischer Scientific, USA).

The cell viability and cytotoxicity of free VRH and VRH loaded HA-CQDs were measured by MTT cell proliferation and cytotoxicity assay kit (Cat. No. E-CK-A341, Elabscience®, USA) to determine the most suitable concentration for further analyses. 100 μL of the free and the loaded drug were added to 5 × 103 of the cells and left for 24 h. The plate was divided into five different groups; control well, Aβ, free VRH, HA-CQDs, and VRH loaded HA-CQDs. The concentration range of the drugs was 60, 30, 15, 7.5, and 3.75 μg/ mL. Then, 50 μL (100 μM) of the prepared Aβ protein was added to each well and incubated with the cells for another 24 h. The optical density (OD) was detected at 570 nm. The measurements were repeated triplicate and the dose that gave the highest viability and lowest cytotoxicity was used in the further analyses.

Both of SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells were incubated with the free VRH, HA-CQDs, or VRH loaded HA-CQDs for 24 h and followed by incubation with the prepared Aβ protein for another 24 h. According to the results of MTT assay, SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells were pretreated with VRH either free or loaded in HA-CQDs (3.75 μg/ mL, 100 μL). The experiment was repeated thrice. This study was approved by Research Ethical Committee, Faculty of Science, Menoufia University, Egypt; approval number FGE223.

To assess the neuroinflammation induced by amyloidosis, tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) were determined by specific commercial ELISA kits purchased from Elabscience® (USA) according to the supplier's instructions (Cat. No. E-EL-H2306 and E-EL-H6156; respectively, for SH-Sy5y, whereas E-EL-M3063 and E-EL-M0044; respectively, for Neuro 2a). These measurements depend on sandwich-ELISA principle in which the provided micro-plate is pre-coated with antibodies specific for the target protein that will combine with each other upon addition of the samples. A secondary biotinylated detection antibody specific also for the target protein was added. Avidin-HRP conjugate was then added and any free components were removed by washing. Afterward, the stop solution was added to stop the enzyme-substrate reaction and the OD was measured spectrophotometrically at 450 nm.

To evaluate the oxidative stress induced by amyloidosis, ROS was detected in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells using ROS fluorometric assay kit (Cat. No. E-BC-F005, Elabscience®, USA) according to the supplier's instruction. The test depends on oxidation of dichlorofluorescin (DCFH) by ROS to dichlorofluorescein (DCF), which is a strong fluorescent substance. The fluorescent signals were captured by EVOS™ FLoid™ Cell Imaging Station and fluorescence intensity was evaluated by Image J software to calculate corrected total cell fluorescence (CTCF) followed by a heatmap presentation of the mean for each studied group.

CTCF was calculated as follows [35]:
(5)
] ( integrated density −
area of selected cell
x
mean fluorescence of background readings
[

The mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was determined by image-iT™ TMRM reagent (Cat. No. I34361, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) according to the manufacturer's instructions. The fluorescent images were captured by EVOS™ FLoid™ Cell Imaging Station and were quantified by Image J software to calculate CTCF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative real time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) for genes' expression
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the involvement of some genes in the development of AD, the expression level of CYP, cytochrome C oxidase (CCO), and CRTC3 was determined by qRT-PCR. Total RNAs were extracted from the samples by RNeasy® mini kit (Cat. No. 74104, Qiagen, Germany) by automated extraction via QIAcube Connect (Qiagen, Germany). Briefly, 5 × 10 6 cells were trypsinized and disrupted by lysis buffer with subsequent homogenization in TissueLyser II (Qiagen, Germany). The homogenate was placed in screwcaps specific for QIAcube Connect to continue the extraction steps as specified in the manufacturer's protocol. The concentration of the extracted RNA was quantified by using Multiskan SkyHigh Microplate Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Then, cDNAs were then synthesized from the extracted RNAs by EasyScript® First-Strand cDNA Synthesis SuperMix (Cat. No. AE301–02, TransGen Biotech Co., China) in a total volume of 20 μL using thermal cycler followed by another quantification for the cDNA.

Afterward, the amplification was done by QuantiNova SYBR Green PCR Kit (Cat. No. 208052, Qiagen, Germany) in a total volume of 20 μL. The number of cycles was 40 and they were performed by using StepOnePlus™ Real-Time PCR system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and each cycle was involved denaturation for 5 s at 95 °C and annealing/ extension for 10 s at 60 °C, besides an initial DNA polymerase activation for 2 min at 95 °C. Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) was used as an endogenous gene and the used primers' sequences were designated by Primer-Blast and they were purchased from Macrogen (Korea) as shown in Table 1 . The results were calculated by using relative quantification (RQ) = 2-∆∆Ct.

To evaluate the possible effect of the induced amyloidosis as well as the free and the loaded VRH on cellular proliferation and mitosis, mitotic index (MI) has been determined. Briefly, Colcemid (10 μg/mL) was added to both cell lines for 2 h. Afterward, the cells were collected and re-suspended in 0.5 % potassium chloride at 37 °C for 20 min and fixed by cold freshly prepared three to one (v/v) methanol to glacial acetic acid. The fixed cells were re-suspended and flattened over microscopical slides and allowed to dry. After that, the cells were stained with 3 % (w/v) Giemsa stain in PBS. The cells were then examined under light microscope (400 x, Olympus BX41, Japan) connected with a digital camera and about 1000 cells were counted for each slide from each group. The percentage of the MI was estimated in 500 cells for each group as follows [36]:
(6)
number of dividing cells total examined cells
x
100

Statistical analysis was done using One Way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) with Tukey post hoc for multiple comparisons and General Linear Model Univariate ANOVA was used to calculate effect size (SPSS version 22). The statistical analysis was conducted using a significance level of p < 0.05, an alpha error of 0.05, and a 95 % confidence interval. Figures were constructed by GraphPad Prism, and Adobe Illustrator CC software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dots
Preparation and optimization of verapamil loaded hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: Different HA-CQDs were prepared using single step hydrothermal method using ascorbic acid as illustrated in Scheme (1) [37]. To fabricate HA-CQDs with the maximum quantum yield % and the least particle size, response surface methodology using BBD was created to investigate the effect and interactions of different CPPs on critical quality attributes (CQAs); quantum yield % (Y1) and particle size (Y2). The design generated 29 formulae as listed in Table S2.

Table S2 shows that the prepared HA-CQDs had a quantum yield % ranging from 8.95 ± 0.78 % to 16.68 ± 1.87 %. According to the highest R2 (adjusted and predicted) with a difference below 0.2 and the least PRESS value, the quadratic model was selected (Table S3). Eq. (7) describes the influence of the significant CPPs on the studied response after omitting the nonsignificant factors:
(7)
Quantum yield %
Y1
= + 9.28 − 0.7075 A + 0.9683 B − 0.7133 C − 0.2458 D − 1.90 AB + 0.2550 AC − 0.3825 AD + 0.9725 BC + 0.3725 BD − 0.7525 CD + 1.84
A 2
+ 1.89
B 2
+ 1.44
C 2

Tables S4 and S5 demonstrate that the regression coefficients of all CPPs are significant with p-values <0.05. Eq. (7) and Fig. S2 proved that HA-CQDs quantum yield % is negatively correlated with HA concentration (A), reaction temperature (C), and time (D). On the contrary, quantum yield % is directly proportional to the ascorbic acid concentration (B). The influence of interaction between the investigated CPPs is displayed in Fig. 1 . and Fig. S3 Quantum yield % is inversely proportional to the interaction between HA concentration and ascorbic acid concentration (AB) or reaction time (AD). Contrarily, the interaction between HA concentration or ascorbic acid concentration and reaction temperature as well as ascorbic acid concentration and reaction temperature (AC, BC and BD) shows a positive correlation with HA-CQDs quantum yield %. Additionally, the interaction between reaction temperature and time (CD) has a negative effect on HA-CQDs quantum yield %.

The fabricated HA-CQDs had a particle size in the range of 3.48 ± 0.24 nm to 11.87 ± 1.5 nm. The 2FI model was the best fitting statistical model as noticed by the highest R2 values and the least PRESS value (Table S6). The effect of different CPPs on HA-CQDs particle size is expressed using Eq. (8) as follow:
(8)
Particle size
Y2
= + 7.38 + 0.5267 A − 0.2517 B + 0.1558 C − 0.8425 AB − 2.79 AC − 1.63 AD − 0.9650 BC + 3.85 BD − 1.07 CD

Tables (S7 and S8) show that the regression coefficients of all CPPs are significant with p-values <0.05. Eq. (8) and Fig. S4 display that both HA concentration (A) and reaction temperature (C) have a positive effect on particle size. One the contrary, HA-CQDs is inversely proportional to ascorbic acid concentration (B). Fig. 2 and Fig. S5 clarify the negative correlation between HA concentration and ascorbic acid concentration, reaction temperature of time (AB, AC and AD) on particle size (Y2). Similarly, the interaction between ascorbic acid concentration and reaction temperature (BC) as well as reaction temperature and time (CD) have a negative effect on particle size. On the other hand, particle size is directly proportional to the interaction of ascorbic acid concentration and rection time (BD).

Design space is a multidimensional combination and interaction of CPPs plotted by overlapping different CPPs influences on CQA contour plots. The yellow area represents the values of CPPs when optimized to fulfill QTPP criteria; maximum quantum yield % and minimum particle size (Fig. S4). Based on the highest desirability (1), one formula (HA-CQDs) was selected and prepared as a checkpoint as displayed in (Table S9). The small values of % predicted error verified the chose models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of verapamil loaded hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: VRH was loaded into HA-CQDs by simple admixing in aqueous media. Table 2 demonstrates the quantum yield % of the fabricated HA-CQDs was significantly reduced from 17.35 ± 1.25 % to 14.18 ± 1.13 % (p < 0.05). A significant increase in particle size from 3.65 ± 0.25 nm to 5.36 ± 0.45 nm was observed in HA-CQDs after loading VRH respectively. The measured PDI value of the optimized HA-CQDs and VRH loaded HA-CQDs was 0.118 ± 0.02 and 0.162 ± 0.014 respectively. Table 2 shows that zeta potential value for both HA-CQDs and VRH loaded HA-CQDs with respective values of −17.65 ± 1.98 mV and − 19.35 ± 2.34 mV. The quantified AE% and LC% of the prepared VRH loaded HA-CQDs were 81.25 ± 3.65 % and 5.11 ± 0.81 % respectively. The LC% of nanoparticles play a pivotal role in their effectiveness for in vivo applications. Optimizing this parameter enhances therapeutic efficacy, reduces administration frequency, improves pharmacokinetics and biodistribution, minimizes side effects, and allows for multifunctional delivery systems. Addressing the associated challenges through innovative formulation techniques and thorough characterization is essential for the successful clinical translation of nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems. Most of the drug encapsulated nanocarriers have a low drug loading efficiency (<10 %) necessitating high carrier amounts in case of clinical translation which could impose systemic toxicity [38]. Therefore, further studies are required to focus on refining this parameter through additional optimization processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro characterization of verapamil loaded hyaluronic acid modified carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: The serum stability study was assessed by incubating the VRH loaded HA-CQDs in either 10 or 50 % v/v FBS at 37 °C for 4, 24, and 48 h. The results shown in Fig. 3 , revealed a non-significant difference in particle size, PDI, or zeta potential (p > 0.05).

TEM evidenced the formation of homogeneous spherical structures of VRH loaded HA-CQDs with particle size 4–6 nm (Fig. 4a). The measured size seemed close to that obtained from DLS data. The optimized HA-CQDs were able to sustain the associated VRH for 120 days as depicted from Fig. 4b. The biocompatibility of the fabricated VRH loaded HA-CQDs was evaluated on RBCs integrity. The extent of the measured haemolysis was illustrated in Fig. 5 and it was found that it increased by the concomitant increase in VRH loaded HA-CQDs concentration. However, all the calculated values were < 5 % which is within the acceptable range according to the new consensus ASTM E2524–08 -Standard test method for analysis of hemolytic properties of nanoparticles [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free and loaded VRH improved cellular proliferation and decreased cytotoxicity of amyloidosis
ce:section.ce:para:
Induction of amyloidosis by the prepared Aβ protein showed significant (P < 0.0001) decline in cellular proliferation by 71.47 and 70.079 %; respectively for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells compared to control. When the cells were exposed to different concentrations (60, 30, 15, 7.5, and 3.75 μg/mL) of free VRH and VRH loaded HA-CQDs prior to Aβ, a clear increase in viability was observed in comparison to the Aβ control group for both cell types. The viability of the cells did not significantly differ among the various concentrations tested, but interestingly, the lowest concentration yielded the highest cell viability. Regarding the used concentration 3.75 μg/ mL and relatively to Aβ cells, it showed a significant improvement in the viability (1.62 and 2.35 folds, p < 0.0001; respectively for the free and the loaded VRH) when incubated with SH-Sy5y cells. Similarly, the viability of Neuro 2a cells was amended significantly (p < 0.0001) by 1.49 and by 2.26 folds when pretreated with 3.75 μg/ mL of the free and the loaded drugs; respectively. It is worth noting that there was a significant increase in cells' viability by VRH loaded HA-CQDs relative to the free drug at the same used concentration and the biggest difference was observed at 3.75 μg/ mL, which was 27.75 (p = 0.013) and 30.98 % (p = 0.001) for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a; respectively as shown in Figs. 6 and 7
.

These results were opposing to the results of cytotoxicity. Aβ group inhibited the cells by 71.47 and by 70.079 %; respectively for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a, which were significant (p < 0.0001) with the control group. 3.75 μg/ mL of the free VRH and the loaded drug significantly decreased the cytotoxicity induced by Aβ protein by 64.99 and by 94.1 % (p < 0.0001); respectively, for the SH-Sy5y. Likewise, the same concentration showed lesser cellular inhibition for Neuro 2a cells (↓ 63.805 and ↓ 96.8 %, p < 0.0001) relatively to Aβ group. Comparing the loaded formulation with the free corresponding drug for both cell lines, the loaded drug showed significant improvement in the cytotoxicity by 83.14 (p = 0.013) and by 91.17 % (p = 0.001) as presented in Figs. 8 and 9
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free and loaded VRH decreased inflammatory cytokines produced by amyloidosis
ce:section.ce:para: Induction of amyloidosis in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells has significantly elevated the level of TNF-α and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines against control cells (3.11 and 6.93 folds; p < 0.0001; CI = 52.58, 85.63 and 35.029, 50.5589; respectively for SH-Sy5y, whereas by 1.22 and 1.89 folds; p < 0.0001; CI = 82.75, 95.27 and 382.29, 484.37; respectively for Neuro 2a cells). Protection of the cells by both the free (66.71 %; p < 0.0001; CI = −77.41, −44.36 and 35.74 %, p = 0.001, CI = −25.26, −9.73 for SH-Sy5y cells, whereas by 11.68 %; CI = −25.14, −12.62 and 35.22 %; CI = −284.37, −182.29; p < 0.0001 for Neuro 2a cells) as well as the loaded VRH (82.29 %; CI = −91.63, −58.58 and 65.97 %; CI = −40.068, −24.53; p < 0.0001 for SH-Sy5y cells, whereas by 23.83 %; CI = −44.77, −32.25 and 47.2 %; CI = −363.71, −261.62; p < 0.0001 for Neuro 2a cells) showed significant decrease in the concentration of these cytokines compared to Aβ group. It is worthy to note that HA-CQDs decreased TNF-α and IL-6 by 26.05 %; P = 0.009; CI = −40.3, −7.25 and 16.72 %; P = 0.041; CI = −15.95, −0.42; respectively for SH-Sy5y cells and by 6.79 %; P = 0.003; CI = −17.24, −4.72 and 14.19 %; P = 0.002; CI = −145.043, −42.95; respectively for Neuro 2a cells compared to the amyloidosis cells. The loaded therapy showed the lowest levels of the cytokines, which were significant versus the corresponding free form with exception for the level of TNF-α in SH-Sy5y cells (46.81 %; p = 0.084; CI = −30.74, 2.304 and 47.05 %; p = 0.002; CI = −22.56, −7.039; respectively for SH-Sy5y cells and by 13.75 %; p < 0.0001; CI = −25.89, −13.36 and 18.49 %; p = 0.006; CI = −130.37, −28.29; respectively for Neuro 2a cells as displayed in Fig. 10 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free and loaded VRH dampened the ROS produced by amyloidosis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11a and c shows that the lowest detected fluorescent signals were observed in the control group of both SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells. In contrast, the highest signals were recorded by the cells subjected to Aβ protein. Pretreatment of the cells with the free VRH showed less fluorescence relative to Aβ control to some extent but VRH loaded HA-CQDs showed lesser fluorescence compared to the corresponding free drug. From the quantitative overview, Fig. 11b and d shows that amyloidosis in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells significantly raised the level of ROS as calculated by CTCF (13 fold; p < 0.0001; CI = 540,290.31, 765,405.4908 and 13.85 folds; p < 0.0001; CI = 340,774.41, 552,982.52) relatively to the control. Protection of the cells by free VRH and the loaded drug significantly decreased the level of ROS by 46.75 % (p < 0.0001; CI = −441,232.03, −216,116.85) and by 75.84 % (p < 0.0001; CI = −668,731.54, −443,616.37); respectively, for SH-Sy5y cells; whereas by 43.95 % (p < 0.0001; CI = −316,679.39, −104,471.28) and by 70.73 % (p < 0.0001; CI = −445,021.77, −232,813.66); respectively, for Neuro 2a cells compared to Aβ group. Noteworthy, the HA-CQDs showed decreased level of ROS relatively to Aβ, which was significant in case of SH-Sy5y cells only (P < 0.0001; CI = −421,944.86, −196,829.69). In comparison to the free VRH, there was a significant reduction in the ROS (54.63 %; p < 0.0001; CI = −340,057.1, −114,941.92 and 47.79 %, p = 0.017; CI = −234,446.43, −22,238.32) by the loaded drug; respectively for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free and loaded VRH restored the mitochondrial membrane potential
ce:section.ce:para: The highest fluorescent signals were detected in the control group of both cell lines as shown in Fig. 12 . Aβ group showed the lowest signals, which was significantly lower than the control by 92.39 % (p < 0.0001; CI = −402,272.15, −188,540.57) and 89.94 %; (p < 0.0001; CI = −349,523.92, −129,339.83); respectively, for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells. Compared to the Aβ group, protection of the cells by the free VRH exhibited significant elevation in the active mitochondria by 4.47 fold (p = 0.046; CI = 1816.88, 215,548.45) and by 4.27 folds (p = 0.041; CI = 4341.58, 224,525.66); respectively for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells. In the same manner, pretreating of the cells with the loaded therapy of VRH significantly restored the mitochondria (9.05 folds; p < 0.0001; CI = 113,155.47, 326,887.04 and by 8.72 folds; p < 0.0001; CI = 123,471.99, 343,656.08) correspondingly for both cell lines. The loaded VRH also exhibited higher fluorescent signals than the free drug by 83.71 % (p = 0.040; CI = 4472.79, 218,204.37) and by 84.36 % (p = 0.033; CI = 9038.36, 229,222.45); respectively for SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free and loaded VRH upregulated the expression level of CYP, CCO, and CRTC3
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 shows that addition of Aβ protein to SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells has significantly (p < 0.0001) downregulated the expression level of CYP, CCO, and CRTC3. Protection of the cells by the free VRH as well as the loaded formulation showed significant reverse for the expression pattern of the detected genes in comparison with Aβ group. The loaded therapy exhibited a significant effect on the expression of the genes in both cell lines with exception for Neuro 2a CCO and SH-Sy5y CRTC3, which were non-significant against the free corresponding VRH. The expression pattern of the genes was presented as a heatmap according to the RQ of each gene in both cell lines as displayed in Fig. 13 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free and loaded VRH enhanced the mitotic index of SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells
ce:section.ce:para: Induction of amyloidosis in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells significantly decreased their MI against control group (↓ 47.94; p < 0.0001; CI = −19.48, −13.97 and ↓ 46.41 %; p < 0.0001; CI = −20.59, −13.93; respectively). Protection of the cells by the free as well as the loaded therapy showed significant reverse in the MI compared to Aβ group by 53.39 (p < 0.0001; CI = 6.94, 12.45) and by 73.94 % (p < 0.0001; CI = 10.67, 16.18) respectively for SH-Sy5y cells. The same effect was observed in Neuro 2a cells where the free VRH (↓ 27.92 %; p = 0.002; CI = 2.23, 8.89) and the loaded VRH (72.57 %; p < 0.0001; CI = 11.1370, 17.7963) significantly increased the MI. It is worthy to mention that the loaded drug showed significant increase in the MI relatively to the corresponding free form by 13.39 % (p = 0.008; CI = 0.97, 6.48) for the SH-Sy5y and by 34.9 % (p < 0.0001; CI = 5.57, 12.22) for Neuro 2a cells (Fig. 14 ).

In this study, VRH was loaded into HA-CQDs for potential protection against the neurotoxic effect of Aβ protein. Ascorbic acid was used as a nontoxic small molecular carbon precursor for the fabrication of HA-CQDs. Ascorbic acid is composed of six carbon atoms bearing a carboxyl group that could undergo intramolecular dehydration followed by polymerization at high temperature to form CQDs. In addition, HA is also a hydrophilic molecule that evades opsonisation by reticuloendothelial system that consequently prolong systemic circulation [40]. Moreover, HA is a vital component of central nervous system and a scaffold component of the extracellular matrix. It also amends the intercellular signaling via the interactions with hyaluronan neuronal cell surface receptors like CD44, hyaluronan-mediated motility (RHAMM), and intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM) [41]. Understanding the various variables and their interactions is crucial for achieving an optimal formulation with desired and predetermined specifications. The relationship between variables and CQAs can be established through Design of Experiments (DoE), a systematic and cost-effective tool that uses a small number of experiments to determine the relationship between process inputs and outputs [42]. Different DoE techniques include Full factorial, fractional factorial, central composite, Taguchi, Plackett-Burman, and BBD [43]. Among these, BBD is widely used. It is a quadratic design that combines two-level factorial designs with incomplete block designs, requiring three levels of each factor without an embedded factorial or fractional factorial design [44]. BBD is preferred for its efficiency in treating different combinations at the mid-points of the edges of the process space and at the center. Its advantages include speed, accuracy, economic benefits, and a reduction in the total number of experiments required [45]. Therefore, BBD was utilized to tailor quantum yield % and particle size by manipulating precursors concentration and reaction conditions.

Prior studies have examined the neuroprotective properties of free VRH in-vivo [3] or in-vitro [46]. That being said, our work is the first to formulate VRH into HA-CQDs and examine its ability to protect neuroblastoma cells against Aβ. As a result, this work is superior to earlier research in that it reported a thorough physicochemical evaluation of the VRH-loaded HA-CQDs and provided valuable information regarding the effectiveness of these particles in mitigating key symptoms of AD, including inflammation, oxidative stress, Aβ-induced cytotoxicity, mitochondrial dysfunction, and disruption of cell division.

In order to evaluate the impact of the experimental therapy, we used two different neuronal cell lines in this study: the mouse Neuro 2a cells and the human neuroblastoma SH-Sy5y cells. This aims to improve the reliability and generalizability of our results when applied to two distinct cell types. Consistent outcomes between these two well studied neural models raise the likelihood that the effects being seen are not unique to one cell line but rather represent a more broadly applicable biological phenomenon. It was found that the prepared Aβ protein caused a significant decrease in cell proliferation and an increase in cytotoxicity in both SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells, compared to the control group. The potential toxicity of Aβ in this research was characterized by mitochondrial dysfunction, disrupted cell division, and cell death. Zhao et al., [47] reported that Aβ had an impact on the integrity of the myelin sheath, resulting in the breakdown of the cytoskeleton and compromising the neurons that rely on the myelin sheath for insulation in primary oligodendrocyte cultures. Aβ oligomers were capable of being taken up by these neurons and transmitted along the axons, spreading their toxic effects from one cell to another.

Furthermore, various theories suggest that Aβ can induce cytotoxicity through oxidative stress, disruption of calcium homeostasis, inflammation and immune response, and interference with synaptic function [48–51]. It has been observed that Aβ oligomers cause excessive calcium level in primary neuronal cultures either through openings in the cell surface or by overstimulating N-methyl-d-aspartate receptors (NMDAR) [52]. Aβ oligomers decrease the activity of glutamate transporters and trigger a reactive response in astrocytes in primary astrocyte cultures [53]. It is important to note that these mechanisms may not act independently, but rather interact and contribute to the overall cytotoxicity induced by Aβ [54].

The use of HA-CQDs in targeted drug delivery to the brain leverages several key properties to overcome the challenges posed by the BBB [55]. The small particle size of the optimized VRH-loaded HA-CQDs (5.36 ± 0.45 nm) is crucial because it allows the QDs to navigate through the tight junctions of the endothelial cells that form the BBB. This size facilitates the uptake of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs by endothelial cells lining the BBB via endocytosis [25]. Additionally, HA is a biocompatible biopolymer; making the formulation potentially safe and suitable for in-vivo applications. HA has a high affinity for CD44 receptors, which are expressed in the endothelial cells of the BBB [26]. This receptor-mediated transport enhances the targeting efficiency of HA-CQDs, allowing them to selectively accumulate in brain tissue. In contrast, conventional drugs solutions often struggle with effectively penetrating the BBB, requiring higher doses to achieve therapeutic levels in the brain, which can lead to a systemic toxicity [56]. Therefore, VRH-loaded HA-CQDs, which can navigate through the BBB and deposit directly at the target site with greater precision and efficacy, represent a transformative approach to brain drug delivery. Incubation of the cells with HA-CQDs for 24 h in different volumes did not affect the viability or toxicity of SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells, emphasizing that CQDs are biocompatabile with no noticable cytotoxicity. This finding is consistent with previous studies that found that the lowest cytotoxicity profile of CQDs can vary depending on a number of variables, including concentration, exposure time, the type of precursor, degree of carbonization, and photodegradation [57–59] and this suggests that the safety and biocompatibility of CQDs should be assessed carefully in a specific experimental context, considering these variables.

However, pretreatment of the cells with both the free VRH and the loaded VRH showed significant improvements in cellular viability compared to the Aβ group for both cell lines. These results are consistent with previous studies that have shown the protective effects of VRH against amyloidosis-induced cytotoxicity [60,61]. It is also worth mentioning that the loaded VRH increased cell viability when compared to the free drug. These observations could vary and depend on several factors such as the specific cell type and the cellular response to different drug concentrations [62,63]. Although there was no significant variation in cell viability among the different concentrations tested, it is noteworthy that the lowest concentration demonstrated the highest cell viability. Given that both cell types exhibited improved viability at the lowest concentration, we proceeded with further analyses using this concentration to minimize any potential undesirable effects associated with higher concentrations. We opted to focus on this single optimized dose of VRH based on our MTT screening, but we suggest that expanding the dose range in future experiments would provide valuable insights. We will be sure to incorporate a fuller dose-response evaluation in follow-up studies to more thoroughly delineate the therapeutic and safety profile of VRH treatment. According to Nageh et al. there is adequate cellular absorption of the nanoparticles to demonstrate the improved cytotoxic effect of [poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid)]-PLGA VRH versus free VRH in colorectal cancer [64]. Enhanced cell viability by the loaded formulation could be attributed to increased drug stability, improved drug release, enhanced cellular uptake, and/or protection from efflux pumps [65,66].

This study also demonstrated that the induction of amyloidosis in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells significantly increased the levels of TNF-α and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines compared to control cells. This is consistent with previous research indicating a link between amyloidosis and heightened inflammation [67]. Preceding studies by Wang et al. [68] and Hansen et al. [69] have shown that Aβ-induced activation of microglia and the accumulation of Aβ in neuronal cells contributed to the production of TNF-α and IL-6 cytokines. Therefore, the increased cytokines observed in our study could be attributed to the activation of microglia and the presence of Aβ in the cells. Conversely, pretreatment with HA-CQDs exhibited anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity versus Aβ group. Qu et al. has found that administering CQDs to BABL/c mice [70] showed decreased levels of neutrophil and prostaglandin E2. According to Zhao et al. [71], the antioxidant activity of CQDs is influenced by their electrolytic environment and their surface functional groups.

Additionally, the obtained results demonstrated that both free VRH and VRH-loaded HA-CQDs protected the cells and effectively reduced the concentration of TNF-α and IL-6 cytokines compared to the Aβ group. Interestingly, the loaded therapy exhibited the lowest level of cytokine, surpassing even the free form of VRH. These findings suggest that VRH, particularly when loaded into CQDs, has potent anti-inflammatory properties. These findings align with our previously published data, which also indicated that VRH reduced the levels of cytokines by decreasing the expression of CD11b, CD68, and Iba1, which are markers associated with activated microglia [3,72]. Activated microglia are known to release different inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, as reported by [73] [74].

Furthermore, a research article by Han et al. [75] emphasized that VRH can effectively suppress the production of inflammatory cytokines in a mouse model of early acute liver failure. This suppression is achieved by preventing the binding of thioredoxin-interacting protein (TXNIP) to thioredoxin 1 (TRX1). By blocking this binding process, the activation of the nucleotide binding oligomerization domain-like receptors 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome pathway is suppressed, resulting in the inhibition of the release of NF-κB and IL-1β, as well as subsequent inflammation.

Moreover, our results demonstrated that the loaded therapy showed superior anti-inflammatory effect compared to the free form. This finding is consistent with a research by Hanada et al. [76] and his colleagues who investigated the delivery of alminoprofen-conjugated silicon quantum dots and observed an enhanced anti-inflammatory activity compared to the free form. These findings emphasize the potential of loaded therapies in enhancing the anti-inflammatory effects of VRH. Overall, these results suggest that Aβ-induced cytotoxicity and inflammation, which are associated with AD, can be mitigated by pretreatment with VRH and HA-CQDs.

In terms of ROS, our results revealed that amyloidosis significantly increased the level of ROS in both SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells compared to the control group. This finding is consistent with previous studies that have shown Aβ-induced oxidative stress in AD [77]. Furthermore, the results indicated that both the free and the loaded VRH significantly decreased the level of ROS in both cell lines compared to the Aβ group. This suggests that both forms of VRH had a protective effect against Aβ-induced ROS generation. This finding aligns with our previously published data demonstrated the antioxidant effect of VRH through upregulation of antioxidant enzymesi including catalase and superoxide dismutase [72]. In addition, preceding studies have also reported accelerated acute wound healing [78] and decreased mitochondria-induced ROS generation by the antioxidant property of VRH [79]. Though the precise antioxidant mechanism of action of VRH is not entirely understood, it is believed to block the activity of ROS-producing enzymes such as NADPH oxidase [46]. Bao et al. found that VRH reduced oxidative stress in cardiac ischemia/reperfusion injury through triggering silent information regulator of transcription 1 (SIRT1) antioxidant signaling. It decreased acetylated-forkhead box O1 (FoxO1) expression while increased superoxide dismutase 2 (SOD2), SIRT1, and FoxO1 expressions. Reduced oxidative damage and enhanced heart function are the results of this protective impact [80].

A greater reduction in the level of ROS has been observed in the loaded VRH-pretreated group compared to the free VRH group. Increased cellular uptake and synergistic antioxidant activity are possible ways in which HA-modified CQDs enhanced the antioxidant property of VRH. HA is a naturally occurring polysaccharide with antioxidant activity [81], while CQDs exhibit strong free radical scavenging activity [82]. The combination of HA and CQDs in the delivery system may result in a synergistic effect, enhancing the overall antioxidant capacity of VRH. This outcome is in accordance with previous findings that have demonstrated the enhanced therapeutic efficacy of CQDs/ curcumin nanocomposites [83] and naringenin loaded β-cyclodextrin and CQDS composite nanoparticles [84]. These findings contribute to our understanding of the potential mechanisms involved in combating oxidative stress in AD.

Regarding the MMP, the control group of both cell lines showed the highest fluorescent signals, indicating a high elevation of active mitochondria. This finding aligns with previous published studies that indicate the cancerous cells retain active mitochondria to evade apoptosis. This is because apoptosis can be mitochondrial dysfunction-driven [85,86]. On the other hand, the Aβ group showed the lowest signals, indicating a significant decrease in the mitochondrial membrane potential compared to the control group. This reduction in mitochondrial activity in response to Aβ is consistent with previous studies that have linked Aβ to mitochondrial dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases through a number of pathways such as impairment of oxidative phosphorylation, increased ROS production, alteration of mitochondrial dynamics, and interaction with mitochondrial proteins [87,88].

These results also demonstrate that cells pretreated with the free VRH significantly elevated the active mitochondria compared to the Aβ group. This suggests that VRH may have a protective effect on mitochondrial function as previous studies have also shown the potential of VRH in mitigating mitochondrial dysfunction [3,89]. Furthermore, when cells were pretreated with loaded VRH, there was a further significant restoration of the mitochondria. This amendment was even more pronounced compared to the free VRH pretreatment. These findings suggest that the loaded VRH therapy, which likely provides sustained or targeted release of the drug, may be more effective in restoring mitochondrial function. The unique properties of nanoformulations such as targeted delivery, sustained release, and enhanced stability can significantly improve the restoration of mitochondrial membrane potential compared to conventional drug delivery methods. Therefore, by optimizing drug delivery to the mitochondria, nanoformulations hold great promise for enhancing the treatment of mitochondrial dysfunction-related diseases [90,91].

Addition of Aβ protein to SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cells had a significant impact on the expression of certain genes including CYP, CCO, and CRTC3, which were downregulated compared to the control group. Previous studies have also reported similar changes in gene expression caused by Aβ protein [92,93]. In the brain, CYPs family play a crucial role not only in xenobiotics detoxification and psychotropic drug metabolism, but they also involved in the cholesterol turnover, biosynthesis of some neurotransmitters, and biogenesis of protective lipid mediators epoxyeicosatrienoic acids (EETs) [94]. Particularly, previous study by Bai et al. reported that deficiency in 24-dehydrocholesterol reductase (DHCR24) causes changes in cellular cholesterol level, desmosterol buildup, and loss of brain cholesterol [95]. Lower cholesterol level are linked to apoptosis, tau hyperphosphorylation, and Aβ production. Recent literature has demonstrated that certain CYP products are neuroprotective including epoxide metabolites of EETs and arachidonic acid (AA). These literature also demonstrated that amyloid plaques can dysregulate the expression of CYP enzymes [96].

Furthermore, the downregulation of CCO and CRTC3 is in line with previous research linking mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired energy metabolism to AD pathology [97]. CCO is a crucial enzyme involved in cellular respiration [98,99] and several studies have observed that Aβ can negatively affect CCO expression. Electron transport chain function is disrupted by downregulation of CCO activity, which has an impact on oxidative phosphorylation and ATP synthesis. This dysfunction plays a role in the energy deficiencies seen in the brains of patients with AD [100]. This effect is thought to be mediated through the direct interaction between Aβ with CCO subunits, leading to a mitochondrial dysfunction and diminished activity of CCO. This interaction can disrupt the assembly and stability of CCO complexes, ultimately resulting in reduced CCO level and impaired mitochondrial energy production [101,102].

Regarding CRTC3, it is a transcriptional coactivator that plays a role in cellular signaling pathways and gene expression regulation [103]. Aβ-induced downregulation of CRTC3 has been implicated in the impairment of synaptic plasticity and cognitive function [104]. One proposed mechanism is the dysregulation of calcium signaling pathways by Aβ. Amyloid plaques disrupt the balance of calcium ion within the neurons, which in turn, activates calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII) [105]. Activated CaMKII phosphorylates CRTC3 leading to its sequestration in the cytoplasm and preventing its nuclear translocation and switch off of the downstream-targeted genes [106]. The observed downregulation of CYP, CCO, and CRTC3 in the presence of Aβ suggests their potential role in the pathogenesis of AD by interfering with cellular signaling pathways, energy production, mitochondrial function, and xenobiotic metabolism.

However, our findings suggest that VRH, whether administered in its free form or as a loaded formulation, can upregulate the expression of CYP in presence of Aβ protein. This effect could be mediated through suppressing the inflammatory response induced by deposited plaques. It has been demonstrated that inflammatory cytokines, like TNF-α, have been shown to inhibit CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 enzymes [107]. Additionally, inflammatory signals can lead to the downregulation of nuclear receptors, such as the pregnane X receptor (PXR) and constitutive androstane receptor (CAR). These receptors are responsible for the upregulation of CYP450 enzymes [108]. Moreover, our results demonstrated that free form of VRH or its loaded formulation, can upregulate CCO and CRTC3 in the presence of Aβ protein. Although VRH does not directly raise CCO and CRTC3 expression, this effect may be mediated by lowering the oxidative stress. [79]. Therefore, more research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms by which the VRH affect these enzymes. The loaded VRH Showed superior upregulation for these genes with variable significance compared to the free corresponding drug.

We have provided a thorough evaluation of this multifunctional nanomaterial's therapeutic potential by examining oxidative stress, inflammation, mitochondrial malfunction, and alterations in gene expression. One of the study's main strengths is the multifaceted methodology used, which helped us understand the various ways that VRH-loaded HA-CQDs exert their neuroprotective effects. For the purpose of comprehending the intricate processes involved in AD and creating successful therapies, this thorough evaluation is essential. Our findings imply that the strong neuroprotective properties of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs are partly due to their capacity to lessen oxidative stress and neuroinflammation, preserve mitochondrial function, and regulate gene expression.

Induction of amyloidosis in SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cell lines resulted in a significant decrease in MI compared to the control group. This decrease suggests that amyloidosis has a negative impact on the ability of these cells to undergo cell division. Previous studies have also reported similar findings regarding the effects of amyloidosis on cellular processes. For example, a study by Borysov et al. demonstrated that Aβ accumulation disrupts several stages of cell division including mitotic spindle formation, chromosome alignment, and cytokinesis [109]. Regarding the effect of VRH and its loaded form, the drugs had effectively protected the cells and led to a significant reversal in the MI compared to the Aβ group. This suggests that VRH has the potential to mitigate the negative effects of amyloidosis on cell division. However, it should be noted that the exact mechanisms of action and efficacy of VRH in this context are still being studied and not fully understood. There are a few proposed ways in which VRH may help counteract the negative effects of amyloidosis on cell division such as maintaining appropriate calcium levels, reducing the production of ROS, facilitating the removal of Aβ aggregates [110]. Additionally, the loaded VRH had a more significant effect on increasing the MI compared to free VRH since QDs can act as reservoirs, providing a controlled release of the loaded VRH over time [111]. This sustained release ensures a continuous and prolonged exposure of cells to VRH, which can have a more pronounced effect on MI compared to a bolus dose of free VRH that is rapidly metabolized or eliminated from the body.

The hallmarks of AD-associated amyloidosis were effectively countered by pretreating neuroblastoma cells with VRH; it demonstrated a variety of complex neuroprotective mechanisms such as enhanced mitochondrial function, decreased oxidative stress and inflammation, repaired dysregulated gene expression, and increased cell viability.

Notably, we improved the therapeutic efficacy of VRH by utilizing an advanced nanomedicine strategy. Our team created and refined the HA-CQD nanocarrier system in order to encapsulate and administer VRH. The robust design of this multifunctional nanomaterial platform was demonstrated by a thorough physicochemical characterization of the HA-CQDs, which included assessment of quantum yield, particle size, zeta potential, and VRH association efficiency. To further confirm the suitability of this delivery method, we also assessed the drug release profile and in vitro serum stability of the VRH-loaded HA-CQDs.

This comprehensive study provided for the first-time valuable insights into the neuroprotective mechanisms of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs, demonstrating their potential to mitigate Aβ-induced cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, cell division disruption and inflammation, which are hallmarks of AD. The study also observed downregulation of genes involved in cellular signaling, energy production, mitochondrial function, and xenobiotic metabolism (CYP, CCO, CRTC3) in the presence of Aβ. The VRH-loaded HA-CQDs showed superior neuroprotective effect compared to free VRH, suggesting enhanced therapeutic potential of the loaded formulation. The findings highlight the therapeutic promise of this multifunctional nanomaterial in addressing the complex pathogenesis of this neurodegenerative disorder.

Despite the significant benefits of HA-CQDs as a drug delivery platform in this work, our current formulation needs to be further optimized to increase stability, solubility, or bioavailability to be appropriate for clinical applications. The next areas of research should focus on evaluating the long-term safety profile and sustained therapeutic efficacy in preclinical animals. Good manufacturing practice production will be essential for the product's eventual clinical availability. AD is an enormously complex disorder, and our findings should be interpreted within this broader context. In future studies, we will aim to expand the mechanistic scope of our investigations to provide a more comprehensive understanding of how our VRH formulation may influence the various interconnected drivers of AD progression.

Given the long-term nature of AD and its progressive nature, it is necessary to evaluate the long-term safety and efficacy of various treatment modalities. Although the focus of our current study was on assessing the neuroprotective benefits in the short term over a 24–48-h period, we recognize that this is only a small sample of the intricate illness progression and management timeline. Furthermore, we recognize completely that although both cell lines are useful tools for studying molecular and cellular pathways, they fall short of accurately representing the intricate pathophysiology of AD as it appears in the human brain. Additional physiologically relevant in vivo models of AD are required to evaluate and confirm our findings from the SH-Sy5y and Neuro 2a cell lines.

To ensure sustained therapeutic benefits, it is essential to demonstrate prolonged pharmacological activity and targeted delivery of VRH. Comprehensive biocompatibility studies, including evaluations of acute and chronic toxicity, immunogenicity, and off-target effects, are crucial for confirming safety. Additionally, understanding the metabolism, degradation products, and excretion pathways of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs will help mitigate long-term toxicity risks [112]. For scalability, robust and reproducible manufacturing processes should be developed to ensure consistent quality and batch-to-batch reproducibility. Adherence to regulatory standards and the design of rigorous clinical trials are necessary steps to demonstrate both safety and efficacy in human applications. Moreover, assessing the economic viability, including production costs and market potential, will be vital for successful commercialization and widespread adoption [113]. Future studies will focus on incorporating multiple targeting ligands on the surface of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs. Utilizing more than one targeting ligand can significantly increase the specificity of nanoparticles for their intended target cells [114]. Furthermore, combining ligands that target different receptors involved in distinct cellular uptake pathways can enhance the overall uptake of nanoparticles by target cells [115,116]. By leveraging the combined effects of various ligands, multi-ligand platform will be expected to achieve more brain deposition efficiency, ultimately leading to better clinical outcomes.

Main strengths of the study:
•
This is the first study to examine the neuroprotective effect of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs.
•
The thorough assessment of the neuroprotective mechanisms of VRH-loaded HA-CQDs, which offers insightful information about how well these particles can counteract important features of AD, such as oxidative stress, inflammation, Aβ-induced cytotoxicity, mitochondrial dysfunction, and disruption of cell division.
•
By creating and refining the HA-CQD nanocarrier system for the encapsulation and distribution of the neuroprotective drug, a unique nanomedicine strategy was used to increase the therapeutic efficacy of VRH.
•
The comprehensive physicochemical evaluation of the HA-CQDs, which showed the sturdy construction of this multipurpose nanomaterial platform, including measurements of particle size, zeta potential, quantum yield, and VRH association efficiency.
•
The assessment of the VRH-loaded HA-CQDs' in-vitro serum stability and drug release profile, which further confirmed the appropriateness of this delivery system.
•
VRH-loaded HA-CQDs exhibited better neuroprotective effects than the free VRH, indicating the loaded nanomaterial formulation's increased therapeutic potential.

Limitations of the study:
•
Controlled but simplified environments were offered by in-vitro models, such as the Neuro 2a and SH-Sy5y cell lines. They lack the extracellular matrix, vascular, and connections with other cell types that make up the human brain.
•
Although AD involves several cell types (neurons, astrocytes, and microglia) interacting dynamically, Neuro 2a and SH-Sy5y cell lines reflected particular neuronal populations.
•
The chronic progressive nature of AD was not adequately captured by the short-term focus (24–48 h). Assessment of the disease's course and long-term implications is necessary.

Prospects for the Future:
•
It is essential to validate these findings in animal models (such as transgenic mice expressing AD-related proteins). Analysing behavioural alterations, neuropathology, and cognitive deterioration across time will yield insightful information.
•
Patient-derived models provide tailored insights and enable testing on genetically distinct human populations (e.g., induced pluripotent stem cells from AD patients).
•
A thorough understanding of pharmacological effects, mechanisms, and safety is provided by combining in-vitro with expanded dose range and in-vivo investigations.
•
More complementary proteomic techniques are recommended for further validation of the gene expression findings.

Pretreatment of neuroblastoma cells with VRH showed beneficial effects in counteracting the features associated with amyloidosis through multifaceted mechanisms including increased cell viability, reduced inflammation and oxidative stress, restored gene expression, improved mitochondrial function, and increased cell division. The loaded VRH on HA-CQDs exhibited enhanced efficacy compared to the free form, highlighting its potential use as a therapeutic agent for mitigating the detrimental effects of amyloidosis. The effective integration of VRH into the HA-CQD platform shows the adaptability and potential of these nanocarrier systems in addressing the drawbacks of traditional medication administration for intricate neurological disorders like AD. The utilization of HA-CQDs as a drug delivery platform presents numerous significant benefits, such as improved VRH efficacy, biocompatibility, and minimal toxicity. This work has established a novel strategy to enhance the delivery and effectiveness of VRH for the treatment of AD by utilizing the advantages of HA-CQDs.
Not applicable.
